n.description: Number of vertices in the perfect graph

m.description: Number of edges in the perfect graph

E.description: Edges of the graph; each row lists the two endpoint vertices of an edge, with the smaller vertex index listed first

P.description: Number of disjoint clusters partitioning the vertex set

C.description: Binary cluster membership matrix: C_{i,p} is 1 if vertex i belongs to cluster p, 0 otherwise

assumptions.description: The number of vertices is positive.
The number of edges is non-negative.
Each cluster contains at least one vertex.

code.python: assert all(sum(C[i][p] for i in range(n)) >= 1 for p in range(P))

assumptions.description: The clusters partition the vertex set: every vertex belongs to exactly one cluster.

code.python: assert all(sum(C[i][p] for p in range(P)) == 1 for i in range(n))

assumptions.description: Every edge connects two distinct, valid vertex indices.

code.python: assert all(0 <= i < j <= n - 1 for i, j in E)

assumptions.description: The graph is perfect: every induced subgraph has chromatic number equal to its clique number.

code.python:
G = nx.Graph()
G.add_nodes_from(range(n))
G.add_edges_from(E)
assert nx.is_perfect_graph(G)

y.description: Equals 1 if color k is used, 0 otherwise

w.description: Equals 1 if vertex i is selected and assigned color k, 0 otherwise

constraints.description: A vertex can only be assigned color k if color k is used.
No two vertices sharing an edge may receive the same color.
Exactly one vertex is selected and colored from each cluster.

code.gurobipy:
for p in range(P):
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(C[i][p] * w[i, k] for i in range(n) for k in range(P)) == 1)

objective.description: Minimize the total number of distinct colors used across all selected vertices.

code.gurobipy: model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(y[k] for k in range(P)), GRB.MINIMIZE)

notes: This is Model 1 of Seker, Ekim, and Taskin (2021).

The paper's symmetry-breaking constraint y_k <= y_{k-1} is excluded: it is a solver-acceleration device that is not part of the underlying selective graph coloring problem (any permutation of the colors used yields an equally valid solution).
